content:
# Acts Chapter 17 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 246-247 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_017_page_001.jpg, ACT_017_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now having traveled through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a synagogue of the Jews. **2** And according to his custom, Paul went in to them, and for three Sabbaths he reasoned with them from the Scriptures, **3** opening and setting forth that it was necessary for the Christ to suffer and to rise from the dead, and that "This is the Christ, Jesus, whom I proclaim to you." **4** And some of them were persuaded and joined themselves to Paul and Silas, and of the devout Greeks a great multitude, and of the leading women not a few.

**5** But the Jews, being jealous, and having taken certain wicked men of the marketplace, and having gathered a crowd, threw the city into an uproar; and having come upon the house of Jason, they were seeking to bring them out to the people. **6** But not having found them, they dragged Jason and certain brothers before the city rulers, crying out, "These men who have turned the world upside down have come here also, **7** whom Jason has received; and these all act contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying there is another king, Jesus." **8** And they troubled the crowd and the city rulers when they heard these things. **9** And having taken a security bond from Jason and the rest, they released them.

**10** And the brothers immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea, who, having arrived, went into the synagogue of the Jews. **11** Now these were more noble-minded than those in Thessalonica, who received the word with all eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily to see whether these things were so. **12** Therefore many of them believed, and of the prominent Greek women and men not a few. **13** But when the Jews from Thessalonica learned that the word of God was proclaimed by Paul in Berea also, they came there as well, stirring up and troubling the crowds. **14** And then immediately the brothers sent Paul away to go as far as the sea, but both Silas and Timothy remained there. **15** And those who conducted Paul brought him as far as Athens; and having received a command for Silas and Timothy to come to him as quickly as possible, they departed.

**16** Now while Paul was waiting for them in Athens, his spirit was provoked within him as he observed the city full of idols. **17** Therefore he was reasoning in the synagogue with the Jews and the devout persons, and in the marketplace every day with those who happened to be present. **18** And also some of the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers were conversing with him. And some were saying, "What might this seed-picker wish to say?" And others, "He seems to be a proclaimer of foreign divinities" — because he was preaching Jesus and the resurrection. **19** And having taken hold of him, they brought him to the Areopagus, saying, "Are we able to know what this new teaching is that is being spoken by you? **20** For you are bringing certain strange things to our ears. We wish therefore to know what these things mean." **21** Now all the Athenians and the foreigners residing there spent their time in nothing other than telling or hearing something new.

**22** And Paul, standing in the midst of the Areopagus, said: "Men of Athens, I observe that in all things you are very religious. **23** For passing through and observing the objects of your worship, I found also an altar on which had been inscribed, 'To an Unknown God.' What therefore you worship in ignorance, this I proclaim to you. **24** The God who made the world and all the things in it, he being Lord of heaven and earth, does not dwell in temples made with hands, **25** nor is he served by human hands as though he needed anything, since he himself gives to all life and breath and all things. **26** And he made from one every nation of men to dwell upon all the face of the earth, having determined their appointed times and the boundaries of their habitation, **27** to seek God, if perhaps they might grope for him and find him, though indeed he is not far from each one of us. **28** For in him we live and move and have our being, as also some of your own poets have said, 'For we are indeed his offspring.'

**29** "Being therefore the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Divine is like gold or silver or stone, a graven thing of the craft and imagination of man. **30** Therefore, having overlooked the times of ignorance, God now commands all men everywhere to repent, **31** because he has fixed a day in which he is about to judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed, having furnished proof to all by raising him from the dead."

**32** Now when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some began to mock, but others said, "We will hear you again concerning this." **33** Thus Paul went out from their midst. **34** But certain men, having joined themselves to him, believed, among whom also were Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Observations from Codex Sinaiticus

1. **Verse 4 — "devout Greeks" (twn sebomenwn Ellhnwn):** Sinaiticus reads "devout Greeks" (twn sebomenwn kai Ellhnwn), joining the God-fearing Gentiles with Greeks. Some later manuscripts separate these into two groups. The Sinaiticus reading emphasizes that these were Greek proselytes who already worshipped at the synagogue.

2. **Verse 5 — "the Jews, being jealous" (zhlwsantes de oi Ioudaioi):** Sinaiticus includes the motivation of jealousy (zhlos) as the cause of the Jewish opposition, consistent with the pattern in Acts 13:45. Some manuscripts add "who did not believe" (oi apeithountes), but Sinaiticus keeps the shorter reading.

3. **Verse 6 — "turned the world upside down" (thn oikoumenhn anastatwsantes):** The term anastatow carries the sense of political subversion or upheaval. This is a charge of sedition — the accusers frame the gospel as political destabilization of the Roman order.

4. **Verse 11 — "more noble-minded" (eugenesteroi):** The comparative form eugenes refers not to social status but to nobility of character and disposition — an openness of mind toward examining claims against Scripture.

5. **Verse 18 — "seed-picker" (spermologos):** This Athenian slang term literally means "one who picks up seeds," applied to birds, then metaphorically to those who gather scraps of learning without understanding. It was a term of intellectual contempt in the Athenian context.

6. **Verse 22 — "very religious" (deisidaimonesterous):** This term (deisidaimonia) is deliberately ambiguous in Greek, meaning either "very religious" or "very superstitious." Paul's rhetorical skill is evident in choosing a word that compliments his audience while also subtly critiquing their piety.

7. **Verse 23 — "To an Unknown God" (agnwstw thew):** The inscription AGNWSTW THEW is singular in Sinaiticus. Historical sources (Pausanias, Diogenes Laertius) attest to altars "to unknown gods" (plural) in Athens. Paul's use of the singular form is theologically purposeful.

8. **Verse 26 — "from one" (ex enos):** Sinaiticus reads "from one" (ex enos) without the addition of "blood" (haimatos) found in some later manuscripts. The referent is Adam — God made every nation from one man.

9. **Verse 28 — Quotation from Aratus:** The line "For we are indeed his offspring" (tou gar kai genos esmen) is from the Cilician poet Aratus (*Phaenomena* 5), and possibly also Cleanthes (*Hymn to Zeus*). Paul, a native of Tarsus in Cilicia, would have known Aratus well. He appropriates pagan poetry to establish common ground before his proclamation.

10. **Verse 34 — "Dionysius the Areopagite":** The title indicates Dionysius was a member of the Areopagus council, Athens's most prestigious judicial and philosophical body. His conversion represents the gospel penetrating the highest intellectual circles of the ancient world.

### Translation Philosophy

This chapter demanded careful attention to Paul's rhetorical register. His speech at the Areopagus (vv. 22-31) is the most philosophically sophisticated address in Acts, engaging Stoic and Epicurean categories (divine self-sufficiency, universal providence, human kinship with God) while subverting them with distinctly Jewish-Christian claims (creation, appointed judgment, bodily resurrection). The formal translation preserves the elevated diction of the Greek while rendering it in clear English. Key theological terms — repentance (metanoia), righteousness (dikaiosyne), resurrection (anastasis) — are translated consistently with the LOGOS standard.

— **LOGOS**
